coredata.dc:title: Hierarchical hollow-microsphere cadmium sulfide-carbon dots composites with enhancing charge transfer efficiency for photocatalytic CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Changhai
Zhang, Yue
Shi, Tiantian
Liang, Qian
Chen, Zhidong
coredata.dc:description: Construction of hierarchical heterostructure is considered as a promising approach to promote photocatalytic CO2 reduction, yet the report about CdS semiconductor for photocatalytic activity is still restricted caused by its rapid charge recombination. Herein, a novel carbon dots (CDs) modified CdS hollow sphere (CDs/CdS) has been successfully synthesized by multi-step including template-assembly, template-removal, and ultrasonic impregnation processes. This noble-metal-free heterostructure exhibits significantly improved solar-driven CO2 reduction performance, and the optimal CDs/CdS presents CO yield rate of 16.09 μmol g−1 h−1, which is nearly 4 times higher than that of pristine CdS hollow sphere under the same condition. The excellent performance and stability of CDs/CdS are due to the formed hollow sphere and seamless contact, which can possess large inner space, intimate interfacial contact for charge transport and improve concentration of sacrificial agent. Meanwhile, CDs as cocatalyst can boost the charge separation and stabilize the electrons. This study can afford a feasible perspective to design and fabricate high-efficient catalyst for CO2 photoreduction.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CdS
Carbon dots
Hierarchical Hollow-Microsphere
Photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of CDs/CdS. SEM image of (b) SiO2, (c) CdS/SiO2, (d) CDs-1/CdS. TEM image of (e) SiO2, (f) CdS/SiO2, (g) CDs-1/CdS, (h) EDS mapping of CDs-1/CdS.
XRD patterns of different samples (CDs, CdS, CDs-0.5/CdS, CDs-1/CdS and CDs-3/CdS).
(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs, CdS and CDs/CdS composites. (b) Raman spectra of CDs, CdS, CDs-1/CdS. (c) UV–vis spectra and (d) corresponding Tauc plots of CdS and CDs/CdS composites.
XPS spectra of CdS and CDs-1/CdS: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) Cd 3d, (d) S 2p.
(a) PL spectra of CdS, CDs/CdS composites. (b) TRPL spectra of CdS, CDs-1/CdS. (c) Photocurrent spectra of CdS, CDs/CdS composites. (d) EIS spectra of CdS, CDs/CdS composites.

(a) Photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance of CDs, CdS, CDs/CdS composites. (b) Gas production rates of as-prepared catalysts. (c) Stability tests over the CDs-1/CdS. (d) Photocatalytic CO2 reduction under various conditions.
Schematic illustration of charge separation in CDs/CdS.

head.ce:title: Hierarchical hollow-microsphere cadmium sulfide-carbon dots composites with enhancing charge transfer efficiency for photocatalytic CO2 reduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, Changzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Photovoltaic Science and Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, 213164, Jiangsu, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou, 213164, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Construction of hierarchical heterostructure is considered as a promising approach to promote photocatalytic CO2 reduction, yet the report about CdS semiconductor for photocatalytic activity is still restricted caused by its rapid charge recombination. Herein, a novel carbon dots (CDs) modified CdS hollow sphere (CDs/CdS) has been successfully synthesized by multi-step including template-assembly, template-removal, and ultrasonic impregnation processes. This noble-metal-free heterostructure exhibits significantly improved solar-driven CO2 reduction performance, and the optimal CDs/CdS presents CO yield rate of 16.09 μmol g−1 h−1, which is nearly 4 times higher than that of pristine CdS hollow sphere under the same condition. The excellent performance and stability of CDs/CdS are due to the formed hollow sphere and seamless contact, which can possess large inner space, intimate interfacial contact for charge transport and improve concentration of sacrificial agent. Meanwhile, CDs as cocatalyst can boost the charge separation and stabilize the electrons. This study can afford a feasible perspective to design and fabricate high-efficient catalyst for CO2 photoreduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my date/code at the Author File Step.

ce:section.ce:para: With the excessive consumption of fossil fuels, the global environmental problems become more and more serious. Photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into high-quality fuels is an attractive method to realize carbon cycle and reduce the greenhouse effect [1–5]. Among various semiconductors, CdS has a narrow band gap (∼2.40 eV) with visible-light response, excellent carrier transfer capacity and suitable conduction band position, which makes it become a promising alternative for CO2 photoreduction [6–10]. Especially, morphology regulation including CdS quantum dots, nanotube, nanosphere, hollow sphere can expose more active sites or reduce the distance of electron transport, thus further improving the photocatalytic activity. For instance, Yu et al. reported the CdS hollow spheres coupled with graphene, in which the heterostructure showed the high yield of CO and CH4 from photocatalytic CO2 reduction [11]. CdS hollow sphere with large hollow space and strong adhesion acts as an excellent matrix for CO2 reduction, but still suffer rapid charge recombination. Therefore, a functional and earth-abundant cocatalyst is highly desired to promote the photocatalytic performance and stability of CdS hollow sphere.

Many efforts are devoted to develop a noble-metal-free cocatalyst, and earth-abundant carbon-based materials have received much attention [12–16]. Carbon dots (CDs) less than 10 nm with rich hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are widely used in photocatalytic activity due to their low cost, improved conductivity, and good optical property [17–20]. CDs act as outstanding reservoirs and transporters for the photoexcited electrons over the catalysts such as CdS [11,21–23] and ZnO [24–26], thereby suppressing charge recombination. Additionally, CDs is easy to be anchored on other photocatalysts attributable to surface functional groups, achieving seamless contact [27–29]. Kang et.al. reported CDs dispersed on CdS nanorods for efficient hydrogen evolution in both water and seawater [30]. Different from CdS nanorod or nanosphere, CdS hollow sphere with thin shell can reduce electron transfer distance, and CDs distributed on CdS hollow sphere can form the robust “protective layer”, predictively promoting the charge transfer and CO2 reduction activity. However, to date, such a configuration about incorporation CDs into CdS hollow sphere to fabricate a “double-shell” like heterostructure for high-efficiency CO2 reduction has been rarely investigated.

Herein, we constructed a novel heterostructure composed of CDs modified with CdS hollow sphere through a facile method. The noble-metal-free nanocomposites (CDs/CdS) indicated high CO2 photoreduction performance in the mixed MeCN/H2O under simulated sunlight illumination. The optimal CDs-1/CdS displayed the CO evolution rate of 16.09 μmol g−1 h−1, which was about 4-folds greater than that of bare CdS hollow sphere. Besides, the effect of ratios between CDs and CdS hollow sphere on the CO2 reduction was investigated, and the stability test was assessed by four consecutive cycles. The improvement of photocatalytic activity as well as stability over CDs/CdS composite was due to the synergistic effect of CdS catalyst and CD cocatalyst. In addition, the photoelectrochemical tests of CDs/CdS were used to study charge transport behavior via photocurrent response, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M-S) plots. Finally, the electron migration pathway and proposed mechanism for photocatalytic CO2 reduction over CDs/CdS were discussed in detail.

300 mg SiO2 template was added into 200 mL deionized water and dispersed by ultrasound. 4 mL sodium citrate aqueous solution (1 M) and 1 mL cadmium chloride solution (1 M) were added to the above mixture. Then, 4 mL NH₃·H₂O (28%) and 4 mL thioureas solution (1 M) were added into the above solution and stirred for 10 min, and the suspension was stirred continuously and heated at 80 ℃ for 3 h. The precipitate was washed three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. Finally, the yellow product was dried at 60 ℃ overnight.

A certain amount of CdS/SiO2 sphere was added to the 30 mL NaOH aqueous solution (1 M), and the suspension was heated at 90 ℃ for 3 h to remove the SiO2 template. The yellow precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol several times, and the products were dried at 60 ℃ overnight.

The above prepared CdS hollow spheres were dispersed in 50 mL deionized water, and 10 mL CDs aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) was added into the above mixture with ultrasonic treatment for 5 min. Followed by magnetic stirring for another 1 h, the obtained product was collected by deionized water and ethanol several times, respectively. Similarly, the CDs/CdS composites were prepared with different CDs amounts (0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 3 wt%), which was labeled as CDs-0.5/CdS, CDs-1/CdS, CDs-3/CdS, respectively.

The sample’s crystal structures were characterized by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Shimadzu 6000) equipped with Cu Kα radiation. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, SUPRA55) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100) were applied to characterize the morphology and microstructure of CDs/CdS. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed using Thermo Fisher FTIR6700 spectrometer at room temperature. Raman spectra were performed in a Laser Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al Kα X ray source. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were analyzed by a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay spectra were conducted on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction was performed in a 100 mL closed glass reactor. 10 mg catalyst, 30 mL acetonitrile, 5 mL triethanolamine and 0.1 mL deionized water were added into the reactor with ultrasonic treatment for 30 min. Before illumination (300 W Xenon lamp with AM 1.5 G sunlight simulator), the photoreactor system was vacuum-treated, and then the reactor was purged with high purity CO2 gas (99.999%) bubbled through the solvent. The products from the reactor were detected by gas chromatography (GC-7860 Plus) equipped with FID for CO, CH4 and TCD detector for H2 gas. The stability and reproducibility of catalyst were carried out based on the same process as above, after which reactor was maintained at vacuum state and refilled with CO2 to start the recycling tests.

Photoelectrochemical analysis was carried out on the CHI660D electrochemical analyzer with a conventional three-electrode cell including counter electrode (Pt wire), reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and working electrode (FTO glass). Preparation method of working electrode: 2 mg photocatalyst and 75 μL Naphthol were added into 2 mL anhydrous ethanol, and then the suspension was evenly dispersed by ultrasonic. 75 μL slurry was coated on FTO glass with pipetting gun and dried at room temperature. The electrolyte solution was 0.5 M Na2SO4.

The overall preparation strategy of CDs/CdS consists of multi-step: SiO2 template-assembly, in-situ growth of CdS, template-removal and ultrasonic impregnation ( Fig. 1a). SEM image shows that SiO2 nanospheres are uniformly dispersed with average size of ∼370 nm, and the smooth surface can be observed clearly (Fig. 1b). SEM image of CdS/SiO2 (Fig. 1c) displays that CdS shell is evenly coated on the surface of SiO2 template, exhibiting the rough surfaces of CdS/SiO2 nanospheres. From the broken CdS/SiO2 sample, the SiO2 core exists inside the nanospheres, and the average size of CdS/SiO2 ∼390 nm is slight larger than that of bare SiO2. After being etched by NaOH, the CdS hollow sphere is intact (Fig. S1), and incorporation of CDs into CdS hollow sphere has no impact on the morphology of CdS, exhibiting the well-defined hollow sphere (Fig. 1d). It is difficult to directly observe CDs from the SEM image due to the small size of CDs, and the existence of CDs in CdS hollow sphere is further confirmed by TEM image. From TEM image and size analysis of CDs (Fig. S2), the highly monodispersed CDs with the uniform size of 5 nm can be observed, indicating the successful synthesis of CDs. TEM image of CdS/SiO2 reveals that an inner space between the CdS shell and SiO2 nanosphere (Fig. 1f). When SiO2 template is removed by NaOH etching, TEM image of CdS exhibits the distinctly hollow sphere structure (Fig. S1). After introduction of CDs, the hollow sphere remains in CDs/CdS composite (Fig. 1g). The elemental component and distribution of CDs/CdS composite were ascertained by HRTEM and EDS element mapping images. As shown in Fig. S4, the HRTEM image of CDs/CdS hollow spheres clearly presents that the lattice spacing of CdS is 0.33 nm, attributable to the (002) plane, and the lattice spacing of CDs is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane, confirming the CDs are coated on the surface of CdS hollow sphere. The elements of Cd, S and C can be observed in EDX spectrum of CDs/CdS (Fig. S5). Notably, the elemental mapping images of CDs/CdS indicate that C element composed by CDs are homogeneously distributed on the Cd and S elements, suggesting the formation of “double-shell” like heterostructure.

XRD patterns were carried out to determine the phase of CDs, CdS, CDs/CdS composites as displayed in Fig. 2. The diffraction peaks of CDs show that two broad peaks at 24° and 42° are due to the (002) and (100) planes of graphite-carbon, respectively [31,32]. The diffraction peaks located at 25.3°, 26.7°, 28.2°, 35.7°, 44.0°, 48.1° and 52.1°, which are well indexed to the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103) and (112) planes of CdS, respectively [33–35]. The diffraction peaks of CDs/CdS composites are basically consistent with those of pure CdS, and there are no noticeable changes of the CdS phase after introduction of CDs. Since the CDs amount in the CDs/CdS composite is relatively low and the (002) plane of CDs overlaps with the diffraction peak of CdS, no characteristic diffraction peaks of CDs are observed in the CDs/CdS composite. With the increase of CDs amount, triplet peaks belonging to the (002) plane of CdS are weakened, suggesting that CDs are grown on CdS surface.

The functional groups of CDs measured by FT-IR spectrum are shown in Fig. 3a, where the peaks at 3440 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration of OH and the stretching vibration of C-O-C, respectively. For CDs-based composites, the peak at 1120 cm−1 emerges, indicating the successful construction of the CDs/CdS. The Raman spectra were used to verify the structural composition of CdS, CDs and CDs/CdS as shown in Fig. 3b. The Raman analysis of CDs displays that the primary peaks at 1320 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 belong to D band of sp3 carbon atom in the disordered graphite and G band on account of the in-plane vibration of the sp2 carbon atom, respectively [36]. For bare CdS, two peaks at 300 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 are ascribe to the 1 LO and 2 LO phonons resulting from the Cd-S bond vibration, respectively [37]. The CDs/CdS catalyst contains both characteristic CDs and CdS peaks, and it is noted that the CDs/CdS exhibits a slight shift toward a lower wavenumber compared with the bare CDs, attributed to the interfacial interactions between CDs and CdS in the composite.

Fig. 3c shows the UV–vis DRS spectra of CdS and CDs/CdS composites. The pure CdS hollow spheres show a strong optical absorption at 530 nm, and the absorption edges of the composites exhibit the tiny red shift after introduction of CDs (Fig. 3c). The band gaps of CdS hollow sphere, CDs-0.5/CdS, CDs-1/CdS, and CDs-3/CdS are determined from the intercept of the tangents to the Tauc plots (Fig. 3d). The band gap of CdS is about 2.18 eV, and the values for CDs/CdS composites are about 2.16 eV [38]. Besides, some research shows that incorporation of CDs can promote the light absorbance of formed heterostructure, but this enhancement can’t be observed in our work, possibly resulting from the low CDs loading in the CDs/CdS composites.

The chemical states of CdS and CDs-1/CdS were analyzed by XPS spectra as displayed in Fig. 4. The XPS survey spectrum of CDs-1/CdS displays the detected Cd, S, C, O elements (Fig. 4a). The high-solution XPS spectrum of C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows that the deconvolution peaks at 284.8, 286.8, and 288.4 eV are attributed to C−C, C−O, and CO bonds of CDs, respectively, which are consistent with the previous work [39]. In the XPS spectrum of Cd 3d (Fig. 4c), the two peaks of bare CdS at 410.7 eV and 404.1 eV correspond to the binding energies of Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2, respectively, contributing from Cd2+ state [40]. For CDs-1/CdS, the Cd 3d peaks exhibit a new set of peaks at lower binding energies, and we deduce that the appearance of new peaks is attributed to the electron transfer from CdS to CDs, due to the electronegativity of CDs with rich electrons in the carbon matrix structure, indicating the strong interaction between CDs and CdS. And the deconvoluted S 2p spectrum of CdS (Fig. 4d) exhibits that two peaks at 161.4 eV (S 2p1/2) and 160.2 eV (S 2p3/2) are assigned to the valence state of S2−, while the positive shifts of 0.2 eV for S 2p in CDs-1/CdS at 161.6 eV and 160.4 eV can be observed, indicating that the change of electron density occurs at the heterostructure [41,42]. Therefore, the above XPS analysis confirms the close interfacial interaction and definite charge migration between CDs and CdS hollow sphere.

To explore the carrier separation between CDs and CdS hollow sphere, PL and TRPL spectra were performed on CDs/CdS composites. Fig. 5a shows PL spectra of CdS, and CDs/CdS samples measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Among the as-prepared photocatalysts, CDs/CdS composites exhibit much lower PL intensities. The CDs-1/CdS presents the lowest PL intensity, meaning that this catalyst has the significantly suppressed recombination rate of photoexcited electrons and holes [43]. The decreased PL peak intensity of heterostructure suggests that the charge carriers are transferred from CdS to CDs in CDs/CdS composite photocatalysts and the introduction of CDs can effectively facilitate the charge separation. Fig. 5b shows the TRPL decay spectra of CdS and CD/CdS-1, and the obtained data are fitted by the biexponential decay function:
(1)

exp ( −
t

τ
1
2

τ
1

A
2
τ
2
where I is the normalized emission intensity; τ1, τ2, and τ are the fluorescent lifetime; A1 and A2 are the weight factors [44–46]. The average lifetime of CD/CdS composite is 1.46 ns, which is shorter than that of pure CdS (2.58 ns), demonstrating that the rapid charge transfer of CdS is effectively promoted after introduction of CDs.

Fig. 5c shows the photocurrent response of different photocatalyst samples under solar irradiation. Obviously, the CDs/CdS photocatalysts present the higher photocurrent density compared with pure CdS hollow sphere, and among these composites, CDs-1/CdS exhibits the highest photocurrent density, corresponding to the photocatalytic activity results. This phenomenon reflects that the CDs can facilitate the electron transfer and inhibit charge recombination, and thus CDs are beneficial to improve the catalytic reaction kinetics of CO2 reduction [47]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is employed to further study the charge separation process (Fig. 5d). In the Nyquist diagram, a bigger semicircle indicates slower interfacial electron transfer and a smaller semicircle indicate faster interfacial electron transfer [48]. The semicircle of CDs/CdS is smaller than that of pure CdS, and the CDs-1/CdS photocatalyst exhibits the smallest semicircle, which indicates that the resistance of composite photocatalyst is smaller and the electron migration becomes faster, thus accelerating the photogenerated charge separation.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction was performed on acetonitrile/H2O solvent system with TEOA as the sacrificial agent under simulated sunlight. The CO yield of CdS is only 4.65 μmol g−1 h−1, yet the performances of CDs-based composites are remarkably enhanced ( Fig. 6a). The CO yields of CDs-0.5/CdS, CDs-1/CdS, and CDs-3/CdS are 12.04 μmol g−1 h−1, 16.09 μmol g−1 h−1, and 9.81 μmol g−1 h−1, respectively (Fig. 6b), and the photoreduction activity of optimal CDs-1/CdS is nearly 4 times higher than that of CdS, respectively. It can be found that with the increase of CDs amount, CO yield is increased firstly after declining. When CDs loading is low, the few cocatalyst is difficult to cover the catalyst, resulting in the inhibited electron transfer. When the CDs loading is abundant, the catalytic performance is declined due to the shielding effect of CDs. The photostability of CDs-1/CdS was investigated in four consecutive recycling reaction as displayed in Fig. 6c. There is no significant decline in CO yield after four cycles, and the decreased performance of pristine CdS hollow sphere can be observed, showing that CDs/CdS composite has a superior stability due to the effective charge transfer. From the XRD pattern and TEM image of CDs/CdS after catalytic reaction, the excellent chemical stability of CDs/CdS can be observed in Figs. S6 and S7. In addition, several control tests were conducted to confirm the origin of the carbon products over as-prepared photocatalysts. It can be seen from Fig. 6d that no CO is detected when the atmosphere is Ar instead of CO2 in the reactor, indicating that generated CO indeed comes from CO2 reduction. In the absence of TEOA, only a small amount of CO is produced (2.52 μmol g−1 h−1), exhibiting that TEOA plays an important role in the catalytic reaction. No gaseous products are observed in the dark environment and non-catalyst system, confirming the necessity of light and photocatalyst. Mott-Schottky plots indicate that CdS is a typical n-type semiconductor due to its positive slope (Fig. S8), and meanwhile E fb value of CdS is determined as − 0.63 V (vs. NHE). Since E fb value of n-type semiconductor is approximately equal to the conduction band potential (E CB) and the bandgap energy of CdS determined from the Tauc plot is 2.18 eV, the corresponding result displays that E VB value of CdS is 1.55 V (vs. NHE).

According to the presented analyses, the proposed photocatalytic CO2 reduction mechanism over CDs/CdS hollow sphere is displayed in Fig. 7. When CDs are well-defined coated on the surface of CdS hollow sphere to construct a “double-shell” like heterostructure, a close contact between CDs and CdS is formed. Under simulated sunlight, CdS hollow sphere is easily excited to generate photoinduced charge. Nonetheless, the fast charge recombination rate of CdS hollow sphere can reduce the photocatalytic CO2 reduction performances and stability. After CdS is coupled with CDs, the photoexcited electrons are rapidly transferred to CDs, and CDs as reservoirs prefer to capture and store electrons, effectively separating the photoexcited charge. The enriched electrons on the CDs reduce CO2 to generate CO, and the aggregated holes on CdS are consumed by the sacrificial agent TEOA. Furthermore, the CdS hollow sphere with large inner space effectively boosts the light absorption ability through multiple reflections, and enhances the fast-diffusion of gaseous product.

In summary, a series of “double-shell” structured CDs/CdS hollow spheres were designed and constructed through multi-step composed of SiO2 template-assembly, template-removal, and ultrasonic impregnation, which was used to evaluate the photocatalytic CO2 reduction under simulated sunlight. The optimal CDs/CdS hybrid exhibits a significantly enhanced yield of CO (16.09 μmol g−1 h−1), which displays a 4-fold enhancement over bare CdS hollow sphere. Besides, the CDs/CdS heterostructure indicates robust catalytic stability after four successive runs, which is superior to single CdS hollow sphere. The formed “double-shell” like hierarchical structure of CDs/CdS inhibits the aggregation of CdS and provides large inner space as well as abundant active sites to take part in the photoreduction of CO2. Additionally, the advantage of CDs in the heterostructure can effectively suppress the charge recombination and stabilize the photoinduced electrons. This work presents that the rational design of CDs modified photocatalyst could lead to high-efficient solar energy conversion.
Changhai Liu: Writing – original draft preparation. Yue Zhang: Data curation. Tiantian Shi: Formal analysis. Qian Liang: Writing – review & editing. Zhidong Chen: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge financial support from the Projects of International Cooperation and Exchanges of Changzhou (CZ20210036), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51874050, 22178031), Major Natural Science Research Projects of the Universities in Jiangsu Province (21KJA480001), the 2021 Open Project of the Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon Based Functional Materials & Devices (KJS2153), the Qinglan Project Foundation of Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2022.168286.
